Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive noun phrase with attributive adjective plus imperfective verb phrase; fixed metaphorical clause using body-part noun 'heart'.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is cheerful, generous, and able to laugh even when life is modest or difficult.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: huwa farḥān w-karīm ḥatta b-zman ṣaҁb
Usage Contexts: daily speech; family praise; neighborly talk; moral description
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in village speech where bread, cheese, and shared meals symbolize hospitality in Maaloula households.
First Example Sentence: Sālim čū ҁandu ktīr, bas lebb-e ṭāb kaḥke b-ġubna.
Second Example Sentence: B-ḥafle ṣġīra, mraḥbi b-kull ġar, lebb-e ṭāb kaḥke b-ġubna.
Third Example Sentence: Lā tḥseb-e faqīr, lebb-e ṭāb kaḥke b-ġubna w-yheb mn id-e.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive body-part noun phrase plus prepositional phrase; elliptical fixed clause describing perceptive attention.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person is watching carefully, noticing everything from a distance.
Usage Contexts: security; family supervision; gossip; cautionary advice
Register and Tone: colloquial, ironic or admiring depending on context
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Evokes mountain-top visibility around Maaloula, where elevated places allow one to see paths, terraces, and approaching visitors.
First Example Sentence: Ḥannā b-bayta, bas ҁayn-e ṣōfya ҁal ṭūra.
Second Example Sentence: Lā tҁmel ġalaṭ qdam Sāra, ҁayn-e ṣōfya ҁal ṭūra.
Third Example Sentence: Raҁyāne qālū: ҁayn-e ṣōfya ҁal ṭūra, w-ҁrefū min ǧāy.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Perfective verb phrase with direct object and source prepositional phrase; semi-fixed idiom built on sensing wind/air.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To get a hint of what is happening before it is openly said.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: huwa fahhem l-amr mn išāra ṣġīra
Usage Contexts: negotiation; gossip; family planning; workplace anticipation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connected to traditional stone houses where a draft from the doorway can signal movement before anyone enters.
First Example Sentence: Qabel ma tḥki l-ḫabar, šqel rīḥa mn ṯarҁa.
Second Example Sentence: Mҁallem šqel rīḥa mn ṯarҁa w-baddal l-ḥesāb.
Third Example Sentence: Hiya šqlet rīḥa mn ṯarҁa, fa-sakket qdam l-ḍyūf.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive noun phrase with adjective and prepositional complement; evaluative idiom centered on the hand as agency.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person is helpful, influential, and able to do good for others.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: huwa yqder ysāҁed n-nās b-fiҁl waḍeḥ
Usage Contexts: charity; community leadership; workplace recommendation; family mediation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects the social value of intercession, practical help, and visible generosity in close-knit mountain communities.
First Example Sentence: Marūn iḏ-e ṭawīla b-ḫēr, kul maḥtāj yelqāh.
Second Example Sentence: B-l-balad, iḏ-e ṭawīla b-ḫēr, w-kalmt-e masmūҁa.
Third Example Sentence: Mā nseyna ḫdemt-a, iḏ-a ṭawīla b-ḫēr ҁal kull bayt.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal clause with possessed body-part noun and locative prepositional phrase; fixed ironic expression of silence.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone keeps silent and refuses to speak, especially when expected to answer.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on a simple physical image familiar in oral storytelling: a mouth full of water cannot speak.
Second Example Sentence: Lammā ǧā sīret d-dayn, ṣār mōya b-fumm-e.
Third Example Sentence: Lā tḫalli mōya b-fumm-ak, ḥki l-ḥaqq hassa.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Imperfective/action verb phrase with object and instrumental body-part phrase; hyperbolic idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To endure something extremely difficult with stubborn patience.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: huwa yṣber ҁal ši ṣaҁb ġāyat aṣ-ṣuҁūba
Usage Contexts: hardship; work; migration stories; moral praise
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Stone imagery fits the rocky landscape and old milling culture; the exaggeration gives the phrase a humorous toughness.
First Example Sentence: B-snin l-ǧafāf, abū-y ṭaḥen ḥaǧar b-sinn-e.
Second Example Sentence: Hiya rbēt l-wlād waḥd-a, ṭaḥnet ḥaǧar b-sinn-a.
Third Example Sentence: Lā tqul ma tqdor; ġērak ṭaḥen ḥaǧar b-sinn-e.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive noun phrase plus prepositional phrase with adjective; emotional metaphor using fire.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is quietly anxious or worried, though not openly panicking.
Usage Contexts: parental worry; waiting for news; exams; travel
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The hearth/fire image recalls domestic life where small embers continue burning even when the room seems calm.
First Example Sentence: Yamma qalb-a ҁal nār ṣġīra ḥatta rǧeҁna mn ṭarīqa.
Second Example Sentence: Qalbi ҁal nār ṣġīra, w-l-ḫabar baҁdu mā waṣal.
Third Example Sentence: Lā ttaʾaḫḫar b-lēlya; qalb-e ҁal nār ṣġīra.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal phrase juxtaposing poison and honey; compact evaluative metaphor often used predicatively.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A speech or action sounds sweet but hides harm, manipulation, or bad intention.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: kalām-e laṭīf b-ẓāher bas muḍirr b-l-maҁna
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects a pan-Semitic moral image of deceptive sweetness, adapted to local village rhetoric and oral admonition.
First Example Sentence: Ḥkā ṣāḥebna b-naҁūma, bas kalām-e samm-e b-ҁasal.
Second Example Sentence: Lā tesmaҁ kull waҁd; baҁḍ l-waҁd samm-e b-ҁasal.
Third Example Sentence: B-l-ǧlase ḍḥek, w-baҁden bān innu samm-e b-ҁasal.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Causative/action verb phrase with prepositional phrase; idiom based on balance and risk.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To manage a delicate situation where one wrong move may cause trouble.
Usage Contexts: mediation; politics; workplace conflict; family disputes
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Likely tied to the image of public performance and precarious balance; useful in describing mediation in tight social networks.
First Example Sentence: Muḫtār raqqeṣ ҁal ḥabl rfiҁ bayn l-ҁayltayn.
Second Example Sentence: B-l-šġel, līna traqqeṣ ҁal ḥabl rfiҁ maҁ l-mudīr.
Third Example Sentence: Ḥki b-hdūʾ, laʾnna l-yōma nraqqeṣ ҁal ḥabl rfiҁ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Perfective verb phrase with possessed object and locative phrase; agricultural metaphor with moral evaluation.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To waste advice, effort, or persuasion on someone who will not receive it.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: huwa naṣaḥ šaḫṣ lā yqbal n-naṣīḥa
Usage Contexts: teaching; family advice; politics; literary commentary
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agricultural terraces and dry soil provide the image: seed cannot grow without receptive earth, just as counsel fails without willingness.
First Example Sentence: Naṣaḥt-e ktīr, bas zraҁ kalmt-e b-tarba yābsa.
Second Example Sentence: Mҁallme qālet: lā tzraҁ kalmt-ak b-tarba yābsa.
Third Example Sentence: ҁand l-mutaҁaṣṣeb, kull ḥujǧa zraҁ kalmt-e b-tarba yābsa.
